examples.title: Shallow object
examples.description: A flat JSON object with mixed scalar values. The default initialDepth of 1 keeps the top-level keys expanded while deeper nodes start collapsed — useful for at-a-glance inspection.
examples.code:
<script lang="ts">
  import { JsonViewer } from '@lostgradient/cinder/json-viewer';

  const payload = {
    id: 'wf_3c4fce',
    status: 'completed',
    exitCode: 0,
    durationMs: 1423,
    startedAt: '2025-05-12T15:30:00Z',
    finishedAt: '2025-05-12T15:30:01.423Z',
    retryCount: 0,
    triggeredBy: 'schedule',
  };
</script>

<JsonViewer value={payload} />

examples.title: Nested object and array
examples.description: Deeply nested objects and arrays with initialDepth={2} so the second level starts expanded. Toggle nodes to drill into child collections — each branch remembers its collapsed state independently.
examples.code:
<script lang="ts">
  import { JsonViewer } from '@lostgradient/cinder/json-viewer';

  const payload = {
    workflow: {
      id: 'wf_3c4fce',
      name: 'Nightly ETL',
      steps: [
        { name: 'extract', status: 'completed', durationMs: 312 },
        { name: 'transform', status: 'completed', durationMs: 874 },
        { name: 'load', status: 'completed', durationMs: 237 },
      ],
    },
    metadata: {
      triggeredBy: 'schedule',
      region: 'us-east-1',
      tags: ['etl', 'nightly', 'production'],
    },
    result: {
      rowsProcessed: 84201,
      rowsSkipped: 3,
      errors: [],
    },
  };
</script>

<JsonViewer value={payload} initialDepth={2} />

examples.title: Oversized payload fallback
examples.description: When the serialized payload exceeds maxBytes, JsonViewer renders a fallback instead of attempting the tree. Here maxBytes={256} triggers the cap on a ~500-byte object. Use the copy or download action in the host surface to inspect the raw JSON.
examples.code:
<script lang="ts">
  import { JsonViewer } from '@lostgradient/cinder/json-viewer';

  const payload = {
    id: 'act_9f2a1b',
    type: 'ActivityCompleted',
    namespace: 'default',
    workflowId: 'wf_3c4fce',
    runId: 'run_7d8e2c',
    activityId: 'act_9f2a1b',
    activityType: 'ProcessBatch',
    result: { rowsProcessed: 84201, rowsSkipped: 3, errors: [] },
    scheduledAt: '2025-05-12T15:30:00Z',
    startedAt: '2025-05-12T15:30:00.312Z',
    completedAt: '2025-05-12T15:30:01.186Z',
    workerIdentity: 'worker@node-42.us-east-1',
  };
</script>

<JsonViewer value={payload} maxBytes={256} />

examples.title: Payload inspector with copy
examples.description: Pairing JsonViewer with CopyButton creates an operational payload inspector. A metadata header names the source event; CopyButton copies the raw JSON to the clipboard so engineers can paste into other tools. This pattern suits fault-detail and activity-result surfaces in operator UIs.
examples.code: {eventType}

examples.title: Primitive and null values
examples.description: Each JSON primitive type — string, number, boolean, and null — rendered inline with type-specific color coding. Useful for verifying that scalar fields survive serialization round-trips.
examples.code:
<script lang="ts">
  import { JsonViewer } from '@lostgradient/cinder/json-viewer';

  const payload = {
    label: 'activity-result',
    count: 42,
    ratio: 0.9375,
    active: true,
    deprecated: false,
    errorMessage: null,
    emptyString: '',
    negativeOffset: -7,
  };
</script>

<JsonViewer value={payload} />
